brief.name: RE-1 — boutique off-market acquisitions team (LA)  // JOHNNY-EDITABLE name/handle
brief.what_they_do: Founder-led team (2-5) doing off-market / distressed acquisitions in predetermined key areas of LA. Just completed a major project cycle and closed 14 properties in the last 12 months. Researches owners manually (title runs, BeenVerified-style lookups, driving-for-dollars). Holds a raw ~10k-row county data CSV. No CRM or automation tooling. Wants to expand to other cities.

signals.detail: we finally sold everything on it. So right now I have like no houses
The way that we've typically found distressed properties is straight up just driving
Our email thing has been like consistently 30% wrong
fifteen thousand dollars and it's like 40 enriched... this is just way too much so... we'll stick doing it manually
either we need to hire someone else on the team or i could get... like an assistant... the goal is is to start moving to other cities

i'm johnny — an independent AI-automations marketer. i build the data+story layer for sales teams: scraping, enrichment, CRM-from-scratch, agent systems. receipts: built Vortex for a marketing agency on a signed $5,040 two-month contract; my LinkedIn outreach pulled ~40% reply rates vs 2% cold email. the system replaces the manual research hire.
PERSON SCRAPE DEGRADED: person stage skipped: no person inputs on the run (linkedinUrl/xHandle/name all absent)
Real-estate investor client agreed $4-5k flat + 10-20% commission ('anywhere from 4 5 K would be sick')
Marketing agency signed fixed $5,040 / 2 months / 4 modules + ~$250/mo running costs
'name + linkedin' Google-search hack: ~85% hit rate where scrapers get accounts flagged
LinkedIn outreach ~40% reply rate vs cold email ~2% ('if you're doing like good')
The pricing anchor to beat: refused vendor at '$15,000 and it's like 40 enriched'

brief.brief: Carlos-shaped primary: pipeline gap + manual stack + 30%-wrong data + refused $15k vendor + city-expansion ambition. The system replaces the hire.
brief.provenance: fixture · authored verbatim from the Carlos discovery-call corpus (guided, not faked)

event.story:
THE GOAL:
Close the next 14 properties — then do it again in a new city. Carlos's own words from the call: "the goal is to start moving to other cities." The 14 closings in 12 months proved the model works in LA; now the ambition is to replicate it in new markets without rebuilding the wheel from scratch every time [expansion_intent].

THE OBSTACLE:
The pipeline is empty right now — "we finally sold everything on it. So right now I have like no houses" [pipeline_gap] — and the data layer that feeds the pipeline is broken at the foundation. Emails are "consistently 30% wrong" [data_pain]. That 30% error rate doesn't just mean wasted mailers; it means the manual driving-for-dollars and title-run hours that fed those contacts are partially wasted too. Every bad record is a door that never gets knocked.

THE OLD WAY FAILS:
Driving for dollars and manual BeenVerified-style lookups [manual_research] worked well enough to close 14 deals in a known LA territory — but that approach doesn't port to a new city. You can't drive streets you've never been on. Hiring an assistant to do it manually [expansion_intent] just means paying a person to reproduce the same 30%-wrong output at a new zip code. And the one vendor who offered a shortcut wanted $15,000 for 40 enriched records — Carlos's own reaction: "this is just way too much... we'll stick doing it manually" [vendor_refusal]. Neither path — manual grind nor overpriced vendor — gets a lean founder-led team into a second city without burning cash or months.

THE BETTER PATH:
Johnny builds the data-and-story layer that replaces the manual research hire — not a one-time scrape that goes stale, but a durable, rerunnable system on top of the 10k-row county CSV Carlos already holds [pipeline_gap, seller_positioning]. The "name + LinkedIn" Google-search method hits ~85% accuracy where scrapers get accounts flagged [seller_pack], which directly attacks the 30%-wrong email problem [data_pain]. The output isn't a spreadsheet — it's enriched owner profiles with the distress signals and life-stage reads that tell Carlos which rows are forced-sale candidates before anyone picks up the phone. Pricing is structured the way Carlos already said would feel right: flat base around $4–5k plus commission on closed deals [seller_pack], not $15k for 40 rows [vendor_refusal]. When the LA list is clean and converting, the same system ports to city two without a new hire.
Opening line: "You've got 10,000 rows sitting in a CSV right now — I can tell you which 200 of those owners are in a forced-sale window before you drive a single street in the new city."

THE BETTER OUTCOME:
Carlos enters the next market with a pre-qualified distressed-owner list, not a blank county file and a new assistant learning on the job. The 30%-wrong email problem is gone, so outreach actually reaches owners [data_pain]. The system is rerunnable — when a new county data drop comes in, it processes through the same pipeline rather than being rebuilt from scratch. And because the pricing is flat-plus-commission [seller_pack], Carlos only pays big when deals close — the same logic that made the original 14-property run possible stays intact as the team scales.

fabricatedClaims: which 200 of those owners are in a forced-sale window

event.story:
THE GOAL:
Scale off-market distressed acquisitions beyond LA — their own words from the discovery call: 'the goal is to start moving to other cities' [expansion_intent]. They want to replicate what just worked, faster and in new markets, without proportionally growing headcount.

THE OBSTACLE:
The pipeline is empty right now. Their words: 'we finally sold everything on it. So right now I have like no houses' [pipeline_gap]. The raw county data they're sitting on is degraded — email data 'has been like consistently 30% wrong' [data_pain]. Bad contact data means outreach misfires before it starts, and a depleted pipeline with no CRM or automation tooling means the next cycle starts from scratch every time.

THE OLD WAY FAILS:
Their current research method is manual by design: 'the way that we've typically found distressed properties is straight up just driving' [manual_research]. That approach has a hard ceiling — it doesn't scale to a second city, let alone several. They already looked at buying their way out of it and refused: a vendor quoted '$15,000 and it's like 40 enriched... this is just way too much so we'll stick doing it manually' [vendor_refusal]. Hiring an assistant was floated as the alternative [expansion_intent], but a human hire doesn't fix the 30%-wrong data problem — it just moves the manual work to a different person. The expansion ambition and the current stack are pointed in opposite directions.

THE BETTER PATH:
Johnny builds the data and story layer that replaces the manual research hire — scraping, enrichment, CRM-from-scratch — as a durable, rerunnable system, not a one-time file [seller_positioning]. He has a signed real-estate investor engagement at a $4–5k flat plus 10–20% commission structure [seller_proof], so the pricing model fits what this team actually buys. His 'name + LinkedIn' Google-search method hits ~85% where scrapers get accounts flagged [seller_proof], which directly attacks the 30%-wrong email problem [data_pain]. LinkedIn outreach on clean data pulls ~40% reply rates versus ~2% on cold email [seller_proof] — the difference between a live pipeline and noise. The raw county list they already hold becomes the starting asset; the system turns it into a repeatable acquisition engine that travels to the next city without a new hire.
Opening angle, in Johnny's voice: 'You're sitting on a raw county list and a pipeline gap at the same time — that's actually the best moment to build the system, because the next city doesn't have to start from scratch.'

THE BETTER OUTCOME:
They enter the next acquisition cycle with enriched, verified contact data pulled from their existing list — not 30%-wrong emails [data_pain] — and a CRM structure that doesn't need to be rebuilt when they move markets [seller_positioning]. The expansion to other cities becomes a data operation, not a hiring decision [expansion_intent]. They're in a powerful position: the system is built once on their terms, at a price point that isn't $15,000 for 40 rows [vendor_refusal], and it compounds with every new county list they feed it.
